scripts.title: How a plumber cut CPL from $120 to $38
scripts.film: Film in natural light, sitting at a desk or casual setup. Look directly at camera. Lean in slightly when delivering the hook and the specific numbers — make it feel like you're sharing insider info with a friend.

captions.label: A
captions.text: most plumbers are bidding on every single keyword. that's the problem.

captions.label: B
captions.text: most plumbers bid on every keyword and burn through budget fast. narrow your focus, quality beats volume every time.

captions.label: C
captions.text: stop chasing every lead. chase the right ones.

lines.text: This plumber was spending a hundred and twenty dollars per lead. Then we cut it to thirty-eight. Here's what changed.
He was bidding on everything. Water heater installs, burst pipes, drain cleaning, emergency calls, routine maintenance — all of it. Wide net, but it was eating his budget alive.

We pulled back and only targeted emergency plumbing in his service area. Fewer leads, but way higher quality. People calling with burst pipes or no hot water are ready to hire. They're not shopping around.

We tightened his location radius too. Staying local means faster response times, better reviews, repeat work. His ads started converting way better because he was reaching the right person, in the right place, at the right moment.
Quality beats volume every single time. When you're running ads at twenty-five bucks a day, precision matters more than reach.

scripts.title: Why 'professional and reliable' kills your ads
scripts.film: Screen record Meta Ads Manager showing an ad creative with generic copy like 'professional and reliable service' — highlight the text as you talk. Then show a contrasting ad with specific benefit copy. Keep it slow and clear so people can read what you're pointing at.

captions.label: A
captions.text: every plumber and window cleaner says the same thing. nobody cares.

captions.label: B
captions.text: "professional and reliable" is what you say when you have nothing specific to say. your customer doesn't remember it. they remember the one who solves their actual problem.

captions.label: C
captions.text: generic words = generic results

lines.text: I see this in like 90% of service business ads and it's costing you leads. Watch.
Every plumber, every window cleaner, every HVAC guy is saying 'professional and reliable.' Your customer has heard it a thousand times. It means nothing.
What actually works is being specific about what you solve. Like 'Burst pipes fixed same day' or 'Gutters cleaned, no mess left behind.' That's the thing they actually care about.
Generic words blend in with everyone else. Specific words make people stop and think 'wait, that's exactly what I need.' That's the ad that gets the call.
Say what you actually do, not what you think you should say. Big difference in your results.

scripts.title: What $25/day actually looks like after 30 days
scripts.overlay: text overlay with key numbers from the script
scripts.film: Film sitting down, casual setup (kitchen or office chair works). Look at camera like you're explaining to a friend. Have a notepad or phone visible but don't read from it — just glance if you need to remember numbers. Natural lighting, no dramatic angles.

captions.label: A
captions.text: 30 days. $750 spent. real numbers from a plumbing account

captions.label: B
captions.text: everyone wants to know what $25/day actually generates. here's what we saw in month one: 18 qualified leads, 4 booked jobs, $3,200 in revenue

captions.label: C
captions.text: small budget doesn't mean small results — it means being precise

lines.text: So everyone asks me — what does a $25 a day ad account actually look like after a month? Like, real numbers. And I'm gonna show you.
We ran this for a plumber. Thirty days, $750 total spend. And we got 18 qualified leads that came through from the ads. Not random clicks — actual people needing service.
Out of those 18, he booked 4 jobs. Four actual appointments where the customer showed up. That's about $3,200 in revenue from a $750 spend. So the math works.
But here's what matters more — month two was better. Because we weren't guessing. We knew what worked, and we scaled it. That's what happens when you actually let an account run and optimize.
Small budget doesn't mean small results. It just means you have to be really, really precise with who you're talking to.

scripts.title: why broad audiences kill local ad results
scripts.film: Film at desk or standing in front of a simple background. Show Meta Ads Manager on screen if possible — walk through audience settings. Keep it conversational, like you're explaining this to a friend over coffee. Speak directly to camera between slides.

captions.label: A
captions.text: broad audiences = wasted budget for local service businesses. your best customer is hyper-specific.

captions.label: B
captions.text: if you're a plumber in atlanta, targeting everyone within 50 miles who owns a home is too loose. you need intent + location + actual problem. that's how $25/day becomes 5 leads, not zero.

captions.label: C
captions.text: specificity beats scale in local services. always.

lines.text: most service business owners are throwing away budget on broad audiences. here's why that doesn't work.
broad audience = everyone in your radius. that's homeowners, renters, business owners, people who never call plumbers. Meta's guessing who needs you.
your actual customer is specific. they own a home, they have a water issue right now, they live three miles from your service area, and they're actively looking for help.
tight audience = less waste. when you narrow down location, homeowner status, and behavior, your $25 a day hits people who actually convert.
broad = low intent. specific = ready to book. Meta's algorithm gets better faster when you give it a real target, not everyone.
Slide: 'Stop guessing. Start converting.'
specificity beats scale every time in local services. narrow beats broad.

scripts.title: Why retargeting is your cheapest lead source
scripts.film: Film in your office or workspace, sitting down, relaxed. Look at camera like you're explaining this to a friend. Maybe gesture with hands when you say 'people who've already seen you' — make it visual and conversational.

captions.label: A
captions.text: retargeting is literally just showing your ads to people who already know you exist

captions.label: B
captions.text: most service business owners ignore retargeting then wonder why their cold traffic costs so much. the cheapest leads are the ones who've already visited you.

captions.label: C
captions.text: people who've seen you once are way cheaper to convert than strangers

lines.text: If you're spending all your budget on cold traffic, you're leaving your cheapest leads on the table.
Retargeting is just showing ads to people who've already visited your website or seen your content. They know you exist. That's huge.
A cold lead might cost you five, ten bucks to convert. Someone who's already checked you out? Two or three bucks. Sometimes less. The trust is already there.
Most service business owners don't even set it up. They run cold traffic, get tired of the cost, and quit. Retargeting changes the entire game.
Start small with retargeting and watch your cost per lead drop. Your best customers are always the ones who've already found you.

scripts.title: Why service businesses win on Meta ads
scripts.film: Film in your workspace or home office, natural lighting, directly facing camera. Lean in slightly when delivering the hook — make it feel like you're telling them something they need to hear. Stay still and confident through the middle points.

captions.label: A
captions.text: service businesses have a built-in advantage on meta most people don't talk about

captions.label: B
captions.text: if you run a plumbing, hvac, or detailing business — meta ads hit different than other niches. here's why

captions.label: C
captions.text: your customers are already looking to solve a problem right now — meta just connects the dots

lines.text: service businesses are actually in the best position to crush Meta ads — and most of you don't even realize it
when someone's water heater breaks at 2 AM, they're not thinking about price comparison — they need a plumber now. that's intent. your customer is already motivated to buy

e-commerce and other niches? they're fighting to create desire. they're trying to convince people they need something. you? your customers already know they need you — Meta just puts you in front of them at the right moment
that's why $25 a day works so well for window cleaners, detailers, landscapers. you're not trying to convince anyone. you're just being there when they're actively searching for a solution
stop thinking of your business like you're competing with everyone else — you're not. you're solving an immediate problem. that's a different game entirely

scripts.title: The hook formula that works for service ads
scripts.film: Film in natural light, sitting or standing casually. Look directly at camera. Lean in slightly during the hook. Use hand gestures when explaining the three parts of the formula — keep it natural and conversational.

captions.label: A
captions.text: every service ad needs these three things to stop the scroll

captions.label: B
captions.text: problem + proof + simple ask. use this formula and your ad works. ignore it and it doesn't.

captions.label: C
captions.text: the same hook formula works whether you're a plumber or a landscaper

lines.text: Most service business ads fail at the exact same place — the first three seconds. But there's a formula that literally works for every trade.
First part: show the problem. Not some random problem — the one your customer actually has right now. A clogged drain. A dirty truck. Dead grass. Something real.
Second part: show proof it works. That's a before and after, a testimonial, or just you doing the work. Doesn't have to be fancy. Just proof that you actually fix the problem.
Third part: ask them to do one simple thing. Call you. Book a quote. Text you. Doesn't matter which — just make it stupid easy. No friction.
Problem, proof, simple ask. That's it. Use that formula and your ad works for $25 a day. Every single time.
